items.title: while, do and for: reject the invalid rule
items.prompt: Which statement correctly resolves this C misconception? The condition i <= count is the normal way to visit every element of an array with count elements.
hints: Compare each option with the exact language rule, standard boundary, and observable evidence in the authored draft.

feedback.correct: Correct. For zero-based count elements, iterate while i < count; count itself is one past the last valid index.
feedback.incorrect: That option preserves the documented misconception. For zero-based count elements, iterate while i < count; count itself is one past the last valid index.

criteria.description: Selects the source-aligned C correction instead of the documented misconception.

options.text: For zero-based count elements, iterate while i < count; count itself is one past the last valid index.
The condition i <= count is the normal way to visit every element of an array with count elements.

answer.explanation: For zero-based count elements, iterate while i < count; count itself is one past the last valid index.

items.title: while, do and for: complete the language rule
items.prompt: Complete the missing C checkpoint in the source-aligned correction. Use the exact token or phrase required by this draft.
hints: Use the checkpoint that distinguishes the valid rule from this misconception: The condition i <= count is the normal way to visit every element of an array with count elements.

feedback.correct: Correct. The required checkpoint is 'i < count'.

items.title: while, do and for: executable application
items.prompt: Read an unsigned integer n no greater than 100000 and use a terminating C loop to print the sum of all integers from one through n; print zero for n equal to zero.

examEligibility.rationale: AI-assisted deterministic draft awaiting independent human standards, security, and assessment review.

hints: Start from input validation and the declared observable output, then apply only the target language rule before considering optimization.

feedback.correct: All visible and hidden deterministic behavior checks passed for the bounded source task.
feedback.incorrect: At least one deterministic behavior check failed; inspect bounds, input state, and the target rule without exposing hidden cases.

criteria.description: Compiles in the pinned runtime and passes every normal and boundary behavior test.
Applies while, do and for without undefined behavior or an out-of-scope workaround.
